---
title: "What it Means to be a Modern Scientist"
author: "Dr. Swati S. Jha"
date: "2026-03-15"
categories: [Reflections]
description: "A reflection on Jeff Leek's How To Be A Modern Scientist and its impact on my journey in clinical research."
---

An idea that has significantly shaped my thinking comes from Jeffrey Leek's How to Be a Modern Scientist. The book emphasizes that being a scientist today is not only about generating knowledge, but also about how effectively that knowledge is communicated, shared, and applied. As someone training in clinical research and having practiced medicine, this perspective strongly resonates with me. In healthcare, research has little value if it does not ultimately reach clinicians, inform decisions, or improve patient outcomes. The idea that clarity and accessibility matter as much as technical rigor has influenced how I think about both writing and presenting scientific work.
Another concept that stood out to me is the importance of openness in research. Sharing data, methods, and findings transparently strengthens trust and allows others to build upon existing work. In clinical medicine, where decisions directly affect patient lives, this kind of transparency is especially critical. It supports reproducibility, collaboration, and ultimately better care.
I was also drawn to the idea that modern scientific progress often comes from connecting existing ideas in new ways rather than starting entirely from scratch. This reflects the interdisciplinary path I am pursuing, bringing together clinical practice, healthcare administration, and research to approach problems from multiple angles. This perspective has reinforced my goal of not only contributing to research, but doing so in a way that is clear, collaborative, and impactful. I aim to ensure that the work I am involved in can meaningfully translate into improved patient care and healthcare systems.
